title: Co-location of skill-related suppliers: advancing coagglomeration research using firm-to-firm network data
abstract: Abstract Firms in industry clusters benefit from locating close to suppliers and customers. However, the pervasiveness of global value chains questions the need for co-location in buyer–supplier relationships. We propose that supply-chain partners are more likely to co-locate if they exchange not only goods but also know-how, implying superadditivity of Marshallian agglomeration channels. We test this in a coagglomeration framework using microdata for Hungary—a small, open economy deeply embedded in global value chains—examining co-location, labor flows, and value chains between firms and industries. We find that supply chains foster co-location primarily among firms in skill-related industries.
summary_en: Why do firms in industry clusters benefit from being near their suppliers and customers? The textbook answer points to goods flows. This paper argues it's also about know-how flows. If supply-chain partners exchange not just inputs but tacit knowledge, their benefits from co-locating should be larger than Marshallian theory suggests. Using Hungarian microdata on co-location, labour flows, and firm-to-firm value chains, the authors test the prediction. Finding: supply chains foster co-location primarily among firms in skill-related industries — confirming the know-how channel. A cleaner empirical framework for thinking about industrial-cluster policy.
data_used: Hungarian administrative microdata on firms: inter-firm value-chain relationships, labour mobility between firms, firm-level co-location. Covers firms across all industries in Hungary.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian regional-development and industrial-policy design (ITM, local governments). The paper supports cluster-support policies that prioritize skill-related industries and recognise know-how transfer as a distinct benefit of co-location. Relevant to EU cohesion-fund targeting and to Hungarian special-economic-zone policy. Caveat: the data are recent and Hungarian; the skill-relatedness channel's strength in other settings may vary.
